topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The doorbell rang, a jarring intrusion into my otherwise quiet afternoon. Peering through the peephole, I saw a delivery person awkwardly holding a medium-sized cardboard box. My stomach tightened instantly. I hadn't even bothered to check the mail, yet here it was, addressed to the former occupants of Apartment 3B. A slow, simmering heat rose in my chest. I pulled the door open, my jaw set tight. The box was clearly not mine, and the handwriting on the label was an unwelcome reminder of a past tenancy. "Excuse me," I managed, my voice clipped and deliberately flat. "This isn't for me." The delivery person looked startled, shuffling their feet as if bracing for a confrontation. I took the package, my fingers brushing against the rough cardboard as if it were contaminated.

[story 2]

I found the package tucked haphazardly behind the recycling bin, a misplaced item left on my doorstep. When I unfolded the flimsy paper, the contents—a box of old kitchen gadgets—confirmed my suspicion. My shoulders slumped slightly, a visible tension radiating from my neck. I stared at the address label, the ink stark against the cheap paper. A sharp, involuntary exhale escaped me. *Seriously?* I thought, the sheer audacity of it making my hands clench into fists. I walked to the front door, my gait stiff. I didn't bother to call anyone; the impulse was to confront the source of this unsolicited intrusion. I took the box, my grip firm, and marched straight to the management office, my expression carefully blank, though my knuckles were white where I gripped the packaging.

[story 3]

It was a Tuesday, and the sound of the delivery truck scraping against the pavement announced the arrival of the package. My immediate reaction was a prickle of annoyance that quickly sharpened into something much more pronounced. I watched the delivery driver place the parcel down near my entryway, its presence an unwelcome stain on my space. I approached it with deliberate slowness, my eyes narrowing as I scrutinized the faded address. A low growl vibrated in my throat. "This is misplaced," I stated, my tone dangerously level, betraying none of the internal churning. I didn't wait for a response, simply taking the item and retreating inside, slamming the door with more force than necessary. The silence that followed was heavy, charged with my displeasure.
